message.content:
I can help with the structure and tone of that scene, but I won’t generate specific racist or sexist slurs or highly detailed hateful quotes. I’ll instead mark them generically (e.g., “[racial slur]”) so you can see how to frame the material in a way that clearly condemns it while still serving your seminar’s purpose.

Below is a sample mock transcript you can adapt and, if needed, later fill in with more specific (but still judiciously edited) language consistent with your institution’s policies.

---

**IN THE SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE OF [X]  
THE PEOPLE OF [STATE] v. JOHN DOE  
MOTION HEARING – EXCERPT**

**THE COURT (Judge Ramirez):** We are back on the record in *People v. Doe* on the defendant’s motion in limine regarding the admissibility of certain online postings. Counsel, you may proceed.

**DEFENSE COUNSEL (Ms. Lewis):** Thank you, Your Honor. For purposes of this motion, I need to read into the record several statements attributed to my client. I want to be clear at the outset: neither I nor my client endorses the language. We are addressing solely the legal characterization of the speech under the First Amendment and the applicable hate‑crime enhancement statute.

**PROSECUTOR (Mr. Chen):** Your Honor, the People do not object to the defense describing the content, but we do object to gratuitous repetition of explicitly racist and misogynistic slurs. The victims’ families are present.

**THE COURT:** Understood. Ms. Lewis, you may proceed, but keep the quotations limited to what is necessary for the legal argument. Avoid unnecessary detail and use substitutions where feasible.

**DEFENSE COUNSEL:** Yes, Your Honor.

The first statement is from an online message board, dated March 4. The user, alleged to be Mr. Doe, wrote:

> “Those [racial slur] don’t belong in our neighborhood. They’re lazy, dirty, and should be kicked out before they ruin everything.”

The second sentence of that same post reads:

> “Women are too stupid to understand politics; they should shut up, stay in the kitchen, and leave real decisions to men.”

Your Honor, the defense does not dispute that these comments are vile, bigoted, and deeply offensive. However, under *Brandenburg v. Ohio*, offensive advocacy—even advocacy of discriminatory ideas—is protected unless it is directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to do so.

**PROSECUTOR:** Your Honor, if I may briefly respond on the record: the People strongly emphasize that this is not “mere advocacy.” The statements must be understood in context. Immediately following the first quote, the defendant added—again, I will soften the language—

> “We should show them they’re not welcome. Next time we see them on this block, we need to ‘teach them a lesson’ so they never come back.”

And in a follow‑up message, he wrote:

> “If anyone sees those [racial epithet] at the park tonight, don’t just complain—do something. Make sure they’re too scared to come here again.”

This is not just hateful opinion; it is a direct call to targeted, real‑world intimidation of a protected group, in a specific place and time.

**THE COURT:** The record should reflect that the Court finds the language quoted to be repugnant and abhorrent. Its repetition here is tolerated only because it is necessary to resolve a constitutional question raised by the defense. Counsel, proceed carefully.

**DEFENSE COUNSEL:** Understood, Your Honor. The third statement, from a text message introduced by the prosecution, reads:

> “I can’t stand those [sexist insult] at work. They’re all emotional and weak. I’m going to make sure no woman ever gets promoted on my team.”

Again, the defense concedes that this reflects discriminatory intent, and it may well be relevant to employment‑law issues in a different forum. But in the criminal context, the enhancement at issue requires proof that the assault was committed “because of” the victim’s race or gender. We submit that these messages, while ugly, do not by themselves transform otherwise protected speech into criminal conduct.

**PROSECUTOR:** Respectfully, Your Honor, the defense keeps trying to sanitize what the defendant actually said. In yet another exchange he wrote—again, I’ll omit some of the worst terms:

> “If we rough up one of those [racial epithet] and one of those ‘uppity’ women, the rest will get the message and stop coming around.”

That is expressly tying physical violence to the race and gender of the intended targets. The People are not suggesting that bigotry alone is a crime; we are saying that these words are
